revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麻織物]][[ko:마직물]]
'''Asa Fabric''' (麻織物, Asaorimono) refers to woven textiles made from {{ill|Hemp|en|Hemp|qid=Q7150699|lt=Hemp fiber|1=Hemp}}. It is also known as '''Linen''' (麻布, Maf, Asafu, Asanuno). Asa fabric is characterized by its stiffness, strength, and natural luster. It has a cool texture (referred to as "shari-kan"), making it ideal for summer clothing. Historically, asa fabric was used as far back as ancient Egypt for mummification and was a precursor material for {{ill|Hemp paper|en|Hemp paper|qid=Q1575415|lt=Hemp paper|1=Hemp paper}}. Types of fibers used in asa fabric include {{ill|Linen|ja|亜麻繊維|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hemp|en|Hemp|qid=Q7150699|lt=Hemp|1=Hemp}}, and {{ill|Ramie|ja|カラムシ繊維|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. While hemp fabric dries quickly and provides insulation, making it suitable for winter use, other fibers like ramie offer different characteristics.
In Japan, both hemp (ヘンプ) and ramie (ラミー) have been highly valued since ancient times. These fibers, collectively referred to as "asa," were used not only by the general population but also in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} for ceremonial garments such as {{ill|Arite|ja|荒妙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arite|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The "Arite" fabric has been supplied since ancient times and is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} (10th century). Asa weaving techniques reached their peak during the Edo period, with high-quality asa fabrics known as {{ill|Jōfu|ja|上布|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōfu|1=jōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110832932}} being presented to the shogunate. During the Meiji era, the influx of Western culture led to a decline in demand, yet production persisted until the mid-Showa period. In the 1980s, preservation efforts began to revive the art, drawing attention in the 21st century.
Linen, a Middle Eastern and Soviet staple, became popular in Japan as a fiber for Western-style clothing.
== Use in Art == Asa fabric has been used as a {{ill|Support (art)|en|Support (art)|qid=Q861259|lt=Canvas|1=Support (art)}} for painting throughout Eastern and Western history. In Japan, asa cloth was used as early as the 8th century for Buddhist paintings, such as the National Treasure {{ill|Yakushiji Kichijoten Image|ja|薬師寺吉祥天像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushiji Kichijoten Image|1=Kichijōten (Yakushi-ji)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11622456}}. This suggests that asa fabric was used in China even earlier. In the West, records such as Theophilus’ writings indicate that until the 12th-13th centuries, wooden boards covered with animal skins were primed for painting. By the 14th century, linen and other asa fabrics became standard, as confirmed by restoration reports of damaged works.
A linen dress from Egypt's Pre-dynastic Period, dated to 3500–3100 BCE, has been discovered.<ref>{{Cite web|author=Traci Watson|title=See the World’s Oldest Dress|url=https://news.nationalgeographic.com/2016/02/160218-oldest-dress-egypt-tarkhan-archaeology/|date=2016-2-18|publisher=National Geographic|accessdate=2017-10-10}}</ref> During the same period, linen was used for mummification. By around 1550 BCE, linen wrappings inscribed with the {{ill|Book of the Dead|ja|死者の書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|1=Livre des morts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q441463}} were common, and by 1000 BCE, depictions of the god Osiris appeared.<ref name="naid120005566844">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木まどか|date=2003|title=日本のコレクションの絵入りエジプト葬祭用亜麻布|journal=倉敷芸術科学大学紀要|issue=8|pages=41-52|naid=120005566844}}</ref>
By the 1st century CE in the Fayum region of Egypt, asa fabrics were used to create mummy portraits, applying advanced encaustic (wax tempera) techniques that intrigued European aristocrats upon their rediscovery in the 18th century.
== In Japan == In Japan, hemp (大麻) and ramie (苧麻) have been used since ancient times.<ref name="せんぐう"/> During the Yayoi period, high-quality asa fabric (大麻) was excavated from the {{ill|Yoshinogari site|en|Yoshinogari site|qid=Q1049645|lt=Yoshinogari Site|1=Yoshinogari site}} in Saga Prefecture.<ref name="吉野ケ里">{{Cite news|title=吉野ケ里遺跡から最古の染色絹出土|date=1989-7-18|newspaper=朝日新聞}}</ref>
During the Heian period, asa garments appeared in paintings, indicating techniques to soften the fabric were already known.<ref name="近世日本麻布">{{Cite book|author=吉田真一郎|chapter=近世日本麻布考|title=日本の自然布|publisher=平凡社|date=2003|isbn=4-582-94461-2}}</ref> Asa clothing was also celebrated in classical poetry, such as the *Manyoshu*: “Wearing asa garments, I remember my sister in the land of Kii...”<ref name="木綿以前の事">{{Cite book|author=柳田国男|chapter=何を着ていたか|title=木綿以前の事|publisher=岩波書店|date=1979|isbn=4003313836}}</ref>
The {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} prescribed that hemp garments be offered during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|qid=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}, reflecting their ceremonial importance.<ref name="週刊神社">{{Cite book|title=週刊日本の神社 No67 忌部神社 大麻比古神社|publisher=デアゴスティーニ・ジャパン|date=2015-5-28}}</ref> Over centuries, regional production centers such as Echigo (越後) became renowned for high-quality asa fabrics like {{ill|Echigo-jofu|en|Echigo-jofu|qid=Q11636489|lt=Echigo Jōfu|1=Echigo-jofu}} and {{ill|Ojiya-chijimi|en|Ojiya-chijimi|qid=Q11459181|lt=Ojiya Chijimi|1=Ojiya-chijimi}}.<ref name="越後上布">{{Cite book|author=吉岡幸雄|chapter=越後上布|title=日本の自然布|publisher=平凡社|date=2003|isbn=4-582-94461-2}}</ref>
== Characteristics == Asa fabric softens with proper treatment and becomes exceptionally durable.<ref name="大麻布復活">{{Cite journal|author=小田城司|date=2017|title=大麻布リヴァイヴァル|journal=Discover Japan|volume=9|issue=9}}</ref> It wicks away moisture quickly, making it comfortable in summer heat.<ref name="木綿以前の事"/> Asa garments also have remarkable longevity, as noted by folklorist Kunio Yanagita.<ref name="木綿以前の事"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Costume|en|Costume|qid=Q9053464|lt=Clothing|1=Costume}} * {{ill|Canvas|ja|画布|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Canvas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キャンバス|1=canvas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q12321255}} * {{ill|Textiles|ja|布|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Textiles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Cloth|qid=Q5849500}} * {{ill|Encaustic painting|en|Encaustic painting|qid=Q308968|lt=Encaustic painting|1=Encaustic painting}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asabo.jp/ Japan Linen Association] * [http://www.majotae.com/ Majotae Official Website]
{{textile-stub}}
